paper_title: DiagnosticIQ: A Benchmark for LLM-Based Industrial Maintenance Action Recommendation from Symbolic Rules

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Monitoring complex industrial assets relies on engineer-authored symbolic rules that trigger based on sensor conditions and prompt technicians to perform corrective actions. The bottleneck is not detection but response: translating rules into maintenance steps requires asset-specific knowledge gained through years of practice. We investigate whether LLMs can serve as decision support for this rule-to-action step and introduce \ours{}, a benchmark of 6{,}690 expert-validated multiple-choice questions from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 asset types. We contribute (i) a symbolic-to-MCQA pipeline normalizing rules to Disjunctive Normal Form with embedding-based distractor sampling, (ii) five variants probing distinct failure modes (Pro, Pert, Verbose, Aug, Rationale), and (iii) a benchmark of 29 LLMs and 4 embedding baselines. A human evaluation (9 practitioners, mean 45.0\%) confirms \ours{} requires specialist knowledge beyond operational experience. Three findings stand out. The frontier has closed: the top three LLMs lie within one Macro point, with Bradley-Terry Elo placing claude-opus-4-6 30 points above the next model. Yet \ours{}\,Pro exposes brittleness, with every model losing 13--60\% relative accuracy under distractor expansion. \ours{}\,Aug exposes pattern-matching: under condition inversion, frontier models still select the original answer 49--63\% of the time. The deployment bottleneck is not capability but calibration: frontier models handle template-style fault detection but break under structural perturbation.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Monitoring complex industrial assets relies on engineer-authored symbolic rules that trigger based on sensor conditions and prompt technicians to perform corrective actions. The bottleneck is not detection but response: translating rules into maintenance steps requires asset-specific knowledge gained through years of practice. We investigate whether LLMs can serve as decision support for this ruleto-action step and introduce DiagnosticIQ, benchmark of 6,690 expert-validated multiple-choice questions from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 asset types. Three findings stand out. The frontier has closed: the top three LLMs lie within one Macro point, with Bradley-Terry Elo placing claude-opus-4-6 30 points above the next model. sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Industrial assets such as wind turbines, air handling units, and chillers require significant domain expertise to operate, maintain, and tune effectively. They are frequently deployed in operationally critical environments such as healthcare facilities, wind farms [22] and large data centers [23, 26], where reliability and efficiency are essential. To support this, many facilities deploy automated monitoring systems, including Internet of Things (IoT) solutions, that continuously capture sensor readings reflecting the operational state of equipment. These systems can Corresponding author pateldha@us.ibm.com Preprint. Figure 1: The maintenance pipeline: IoT sensors rule-based alarms corrective actions. Figure 2: End-to-end workflow of dataset generation pipeline. detect anomalies by evaluating predefined conditions against sensor data, but they generally provide limited guidance on what corrective action technician should take once an issue is identified [10, 25]. Consider Bob, facility manager responsible for HVAC systems in data center. Bob authors rules that raise alerts based on asset-specific logical conditions, such as Temperature > 80F (Condition 1) or Enthalpy < 15 BTU/lb (Condition 2), that indicate potential faults (Figure 1). When these conditions trigger, alerts are sent to Alice, an operator, who is expected to act, for example by inspecting valves, belts, or alignment. The bottleneck is not detection, but the action: The rules that translate raw sensor data into triggered conditions are already authored by domain experts using standard guidelines [3]; the open problem is deciding what to do once rule fires. Determining the right inspection or repair step often exceeds Bobs expertise, and the challenge compounds in environments with heterogeneous assets from different manufacturers, with different operating modes and hundreds of sensors. For each abnormal condition, identifying what to inspect or repair requires asset-specific knowledge of failure modes typically gained through years of hands-on experience. Can LLMs help bridge this gap? In our human evaluation, frontier LLMs answered 10 of 12 questions on which no engineering practitioner in our panel could identify the correct response (Appendix Section C; although at N=40, no LLM significantly exceeds the best human). Scope of this work: We do not study LLMs as time series reasoners [15, 38]. Instead, we assume the symbolic rule and its triggering condition as input, and study LLMs as decision support for the rule-to-action step: given triggered symbolic condition on known asset, recommend an appropriate maintenance action. This is complementary to growing line of work on LLMs for rule discovery from operational data [46], predictive maintenance and signal monitoring [4, 9, 14, 29, 34, 36], but addresses distinct downstream question: once rule has fired, what should the technician do? Despite the promise of LLMs for this task [49], systematic evaluation has been hindered by the lack of realistic, standardized benchmarks. To address this gap, we present DiagnosticIQ, benchmark suite for industrial maintenance action recommendation. Figure 2 outlines its key components. Grounded in real-world rules and expertvalidated actions, the suite features primary multiple-choice question-answering (MCQA) dataset along with several variants, each evaluating specific capability such as reasoning, generalization, or robustness. Our contributions are: Symbolic-to-MCQA pipeline. deterministic pipeline maps expert-authored symbolic rules, including temporal predicates, into MCQA. Rules are normalized into Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF) to expand rule-to-action coverage, and an embedding-based sampling mechanism constructs diverse distribution of plausible distractors. DiagnosticIQ benchmark. We build DiagnosticIQ, benchmark of 6,690 expert-validated multiple-choice questions derived from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 heterogeneous asset types. Extensive empirical evaluation. We benchmark 29 LLMs across 9 families and 4 embedding baselines on DiagnosticIQ and five targeted variants (DiagnosticIQ Pro, DiagnosticIQ Aug, DiagnosticIQ Verbose, DiagnosticIQ Pert, DiagnosticIQ Rationale), with analyses spanning question complexity, asset type, robustness, fine-tuning, rationale validation, and operational impact. Summary of Empirical Insights. DiagnosticIQ remains unsaturated for current frontier models. The leading model reaches 73.6% Macro and loses 13.8 points on DiagnosticIQ Pro (hard dataset). The three latest-generation closed-source releases (claude-opus-4-6, gpt-5.4, gemini-3.1) cluster within one point of each other. Note that model selection was determined in part by our monthly compute budget ($5000) alongside model availability and family coverage priorities. Beyond aggregate accuracy, two failure modes emerge. logical-inversion probe shows the leader selects the now-incorrect original answer 63% of the time, indicating that current LLMs treat structured rules as templates to recognize rather than conditions to evaluate. Paraphrasing rules into natural language costs 4.3 Macro points on average (up to 9.6 for smaller models), exposing symbolic-to-linguistic gap. Two further findings bear on deployment: fine-tuning bifurcates by base architecture (SFT favors reasoning-tuned models; GRPO favors non-reasoning ones), and correct recommendations from the top model account for an estimated USD 3.134.82M in operational impact.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Distinguishing genuine reasoning from surface pattern matching motivates ongoing work on benchmark robustness, contamination detection, and evaluation methodology [17, 32, 43, 47, 48]. DiagnosticIQ contributes by introducing benchmark whose templated structure enables direct probes of logical sensitivity and by extending rationale-based evaluation to industrial maintenance [37]. Table 1 situates DiagnosticIQ against nine widely-cited MCQA benchmarks along four dimensions: Industrial-asset Specificity (IS), concrete physical assets and failure modes; Option Distractors (OD), plausible rather than random; Condition Understanding (CU), parsing compositional logical conditions; and Action Recommendation (AR), answering with maintenance action rather than fact recall. No prior benchmark covers all four; DiagnosticIQ is the first to do so. Table 1: MCQA benchmark comparison. Benchmark IS OD CU AR GPQA [31] TruthfulQA [19] MMLU [12] MMLU-Pro [39] ComplexBench [40] CAMB [44] Wind Turbine [21] PHM-Bench [42] FailureSensorIQ [8] DiagnosticIQ (Ours) Industrial-asset Specific (IS) benchmarks: Domain-grounded QA datasets exist for telecommunications [16], climate [33], finance [7], healthcare [28, 37], IT operations [45], and power plants [13]. Within industrial maintenance, recent benchmarks include CAMB [44] for civil aviation, windturbine log classification benchmark [21], PHM-Bench [42] for code-generation prognostics across 18 asset classes, and FailureSensorIQ [8] for sensor-failure-mode association covering 10 asset classes. These benchmarks evaluate recognition (identifying fault or its sensor signature) rather than action selection once fault is detected. DiagnosticIQ targets the latter. Option Distractors (OD): MCQA reliability depends on distractor quality. MMLU-Pro [39] expands the option set to ten plausible candidates in general-knowledge setting, and FailureSensorIQ [8] adopts similar strategy industrially. Neither targets compositional rule conditions or maintenance actions. DiagnosticIQ extends this approach by sampling distractors from operationally adjacent actions within the same asset family. Condition Understanding (CU): Compositional instruction following has been studied at scale by ComplexBench [40], which evaluates Selection, Chain, and conjunctive instruction structures. ComplexBench is closest in spirit but is not industrial: it lacks sensor predicates, asset-specific terminology, and the temporal duration constraints characteristic of maintenance rules. Since rules effectively encode domain knowledge [46] and are widely deployed in production systems such as Oracle Maintenance Cloud [24], DiagnosticIQ embeds compositional industrial rules directly into the question. Action Recommendation (AR): substantial body of NLP-for-maintenance work addresses fault recognition, log classification, and sensor-failure association. Upstream pipelines for rule discovery [46] and predictive maintenance [4, 9, 14, 29, 34, 36] address detection. The downstream rule-to-action step, deciding which maintenance action technician should take when rule fires, has not been the subject of standardized benchmark. DiagnosticIQ fills this gap, complementing rather than replacing the deterministic rule execution that handles known cases. 3 MCQA robustness and cross-asset transfer: Prior work addresses MCQA biases [17, 32, 43, 47, 48] and rationale evaluation in healthcare [37]. DiagnosticIQ extends both to industrial maintenance and additionally evaluates rule-to-action transfer across 16 asset types.

sections.title: 3 Symbolic Conditions to MCQA
sections.content: We present pipeline that converts single expert-authored rule into multiple grounded multiplechoice questions. The pipeline composes four ingredients into single deterministic procedure: (i) parsing rule documents into structured condition trees with associated actions and metadata; (ii) normalizing trees into Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF) so that each conjunction yields logically valid trigger scenario; (iii) generating two complementary question types, selection and elimination, each drawing distractors from rules at opposite ends of rule-rule semantic similarity scale; and (iv) preserving asset-level metadata (asset name, description, triggering duration) in the prompt so that questions are answerable only by reasoning over both rule and asset context. While each component is established, their composition into deterministic rule-to-MCQA pipeline is, to our knowledge, novel for industrial maintenance. Figure 3: Symbolic Conditions to MCQA Pipeline. Met for 2 Hours applies to any condition. 3.1 Input: Expert-Curated Rule Documents The rule documents originated from the Smarter Buildings initiative. Reliability Engineers, System Administrators, and Rules Logic committee collaboratively developed the fault-detection logic using ASHRAE guidelines [3], refining it iteratively over several years across multiple equipment types (e.g., Air Handlers, Chillers, Boilers) to expand coverage and maintain diagnostic accuracy. detailed description of the rule development process is provided in Appendix A.2. As shown in Figure 3, each rule Ri comprises three components: Conditions: set Ci = ci of atomic boolean predicates over sensor readings or asset states (e.g., Temperature > 80F, Enthalpy < 15 BTU/lb), which must be jointly satisfied for duration ti to activate the rule. The duration ti is preserved as an explicit field in the question prompt (Section 3.2.1) so that temporal sensitivity is testable. Maintenance actions: set Oi = {oi rule triggers. }, each hypothesized for verification once the 2, . . . , oi 2, . . . , ci 1, oi 1, ci Metadata: Rule identifier (rule_id), rule name, asset type (asset_type), rule description, and estimated cost savings achieved by applying the rule. We extract and assemble these conditions into structured logical formula, the condition tree Ri: boolean expression constructed from Ci using , , and optionally , where each leaf is an atomic condition and the root evaluates to True iff the rule fires. We define rule as the tuple Ri = (rule_id, asset_type, Ri, Oi, Ci, ti), and the full corpus as DS = {Ri}NR i=1 with NR = 118. Rules span 16 asset types (Appendix Table 5 summarizes the per-asset count of rules, disjunction operators, atomic conditions, and observations). The number of disjunctions is informative because it bounds the branching complexity of Ri and therefore the number of distinct trigger scenarios derivable from single rule. 4 Asset descriptions: Industrial assets of the same type can differ substantially in configuration (e.g., horizontal vs. vertical pumps, manufacturer, age, sensor suite). To anchor questions in this context, we developed concise textual descriptions Desc for each asset type in collaboration with industry experts (Appendix Table 4), inserted into the prompt as the AD field. An ablation in Appendix A.7 shows that removing AD reduces accuracy by 8.610.2% across three models, confirming that asset context is load-bearing for this task and not presentational addition.

sections.title: 3.2 QA Generation Pipeline
sections.content: We design two complementary question types that probe different reasoning skills. Selection questions ask the model to identify the most relevant action given the question conditions QC, testing whether the model can prioritize the correct root cause from operationally adjacent alternatives. Elimination questions invert this: the model must identify the action that does not belong to Ri, testing whether it can recognize when condition-action mapping is unsupported. The two types share question structure but use opposite distractor-sampling strategies, exposing the model to complementary failure modes. Before question generation, we precompute the two artifacts. Rule-Rule Similarity (RRSim): For each rule Ri, we build textual representation of its condition tree Ri from the expert documentation, embed each representation with all-mpnet-base-v2 [30], and compute pairwise cosine similarities. RRSim allows us to retrieve semantically similar or dissimilar rules at distractor-sampling time, which is the lever that makes selection and elimination probes inequivalent. The full RRSim matrix and analysis are given in Appendix Section A.5.1. Unique Observations (UO): We manually curate and categorize observations and actions across DS into universal candidate pool. The benchmark contains O = 193 unique observations with mean length of approximately 20 tokens; four examples appear in Figure 3 under Options, and the full word-count distribution is given in Appendix Figure 8.

sections.title: 3.2.1 Question-Answer Structure
sections.content: Each question Qi is tuple (AD, QC, QP, OP T, A), where AD is the asset name and description, QC specifies the observed conditions for the question (including the triggering duration ti where applicable), QP is the question prompt drawn from NQT templates, OP is the set of answer options (4 in DiagnosticIQ, 10 in DiagnosticIQ-Pro), and is the ground-truth answer (single-correct).

sections.title: 3.2.2 Rule Representation to Disjunctive Normal Form
sections.content: (cid:17) k=1 (cid:16)(cid:86)mk j=1 ci kj For each rule Ri, we convert the condition tree Ri into Disjunctive Normal Form, Ri (cid:87)K DNF = , where each conjunctive clause is complete observation pattern sufficient to activate the rule. This yields distinct, logically valid trigger scenarios per rule, each used as an independent QC, rather than single example per rule. For example, rule R0 = (c1 c2) c3 expands to DNF (c1 c3) (c2 c3), yielding two questions. We use conjunctive (DNF-expanded) presentation rather than the full condition tree because it matches how rules execute in the production monitoring system, where technicians see one fired conjunction at time. An ablation study in Appendix Section A.13 confirms that condition-tree presentation yields only small accuracy gain (12 points) without changing the leaderboard ordering.

sections.title: 3.2.3 Selecting Sets of Actions
sections.content: For selection question, we retrieve the Nsel_topk rules least similar to Ri under RRSim and collect j}Ninc their observations into IN = {inci j=1 as candidate distractors. We then form answer tuples Oi as the correct option, (ii) sampling α incorrect {(QP j=1 by (i) taking each oi options from IN Oi, and (iii) composing the prompt QP from template pool of size NQT . Sampling distractors from dissimilar rules ensures they are operationally adjacent (same general failure family) without being semantically correct alternatives. , OP j)}Nsel , Ai For an elimination question, the polarity inverts: the correct option is one that does not belong to Ri. We retrieve the Nele_topk rules least similar to Ri and collect their observations as CORi = j=1 . We sample min(Ncor, β) observations from CORi as correct options, and pair each {cori j}Ncor 5 with incorrect options drawn from Oi. This forces the model to recognize which actions are not supported by the rules conditions, complementary skill to selection. The hyper-parameters α and β control the number of distractors sampled per rule for selection and elimination, respectively. Larger values increase question count but also increase option overlap across questions derived from the same rule. We use α = β = 10 throughout, selected as the elbow of the size-vs-diversity curve (Appendix Section A.4).

sections.title: 4 DiagnosticIQ
sections.content: We apply the pipeline described in Section 3 on 118 expert-curated rules to construct DiagnosticIQ. We set the hyper-parameters Nsel_topk = 25, Neli_topk = 25, NQT = 10, α = 10, and β = 10. The resulting dataset contains 6690 questions, with composition shown in Figure 4. Selection questions account for 77.4% of the dataset and elimination questions for the remaining 22.6%. The predominance of selection questions is structural consequence of the pipeline: incorrect options for elimination questions are sampled from Oi for given Ri, which is small relative to the cross-rule pool used for selection distractors. Setting α β would rebalance the ratio but at the cost of generating many near-duplicate elimination questions, reducing diversity. Figure 4: DiagnosticIQ Pro composition by asset type (outer ring), option count (inner ring), and question type. By asset type, DiagnosticIQ is dominated by Air Handling Unit (AHU) scenarios (58%), followed by Chiller and Boiler (7% and 6% respectively). This skew is intentional and reflects operational reality: AHUs are the most prevalent equipment at most facility sites and account for the largest share of energy savings opportunities, so domain experts authored proportionally more rules for them. Rather than resampling to equalize coverage, we preserve the imbalance so that benchmark performance reflects the operational distribution practitioners actually face. Similar dataset imbalance is documented in other domain benchmarks such as GPQA [31] and HLE dataset [6]. second structural property of the dataset is variable option count for elimination questions. Selection questions have fixed option set; elimination questions draw their incorrect options from the source rules own action set Oi, which varies in size across rules. We report this transparently rather than truncate, since rule-specific action-set size is itself diagnostic information about the asset.

sections.title: 4.1 Variants for Targeted Evaluation
sections.content: DiagnosticIQ Pro: higher-difficulty variant of DiagnosticIQ. We expand each question from 4 to 10 answer options to stress-test discrimination among many plausible distractors. The 10-option design follows FailureSensorIQ [8] and MMLU-Pro [39]. DiagnosticIQ Pert: robustness variant of DiagnosticIQ Pro. We apply PertEval [17] to perturb each questions surface form (option ordering, wording) while preserving semantics, isolating sensitivity to formatting and position bias from reasoning ability. Construction details in Appendix A.6.2. DiagnosticIQ Rationale: reasoning-trace variant of DiagnosticIQ. For each question, we collect the chain-of-thought rationale the model produces alongside its answer. Domain experts rate these rationales on knowledge accuracy and operational relevance (Section 5.2). The rationales also serve as supervision signal for the fine-tuning experiments (Section 5.3). DiagnosticIQ Verbose: linguistic-paraphrase variant of DiagnosticIQ. Symbolic question conditions (QC) are rewritten into natural language. Comparing DiagnosticIQ against DiagnosticIQ Verbose isolates the symbolic-to-linguistic gap. Construction details in Appendix A.6.3. DiagnosticIQ Aug: logical-stress variant inverting all temporal comparison operators (<>, ) and adding None of the above as new option. It helps in isolating sensitivity to logical content from rule-shape recognition. Manual verification is conducted to check validity.

sections.title: 5 Experimental Results
sections.content: We evaluate models via direct zero-shot prompting (representative prompts in Appendix Figure 15; inference parameters in Appendix A.16). Given the asset-class imbalance in DiagnosticIQ, we report both standard accuracy and macro-averaged accuracy: Acc = 1 DQ xDQ (cid:88) [M (q(x))=yx], Accmacro = 1 (cid:88) aA 1 Da xDa (cid:88) [M (q(x))=yx], where q(x) is the generated prompt, () the models response, [] the indicator, DQ the full dataset, the set of asset classes, and Da the questions for asset a.

The claude-opus-4-6 loses 13.78 Macro points moving from DiagnosticIQ to DiagnosticIQ Pro (73.59% 59.81%). Only 5 of 29 models clear 50% on DiagnosticIQ Pro. Across the full population, relative accuracy drops range from 19% (frontier) to over 60% (smaller models), comparable in magnitude to the 1633% drops on MMLU-Pro [39]. The drop confirms that distractor-set expansion is transferable lever for exposing frontier brittleness. Many LLMs fall back on lexical similarity under distractor pressure. For each question, we embed the question stem (AD, QC, QP ) and each option, then select the option with highest cosine similarity to the stem. The strongest baseline, all-mpnet-base-v2, reaches 52.73% Macro on DiagnosticIQ and 38.89% on DiagnosticIQ Pro. While this trails every LLM on DiagnosticIQ, it surpasses 14 of 29 LLMs on DiagnosticIQ Pro, including reasoning models (o1) and large open7 weight models (llama-3-1-405b, gpt-oss-120b). Half the leaderboard cannot beat unsupervised cosine similarity once the distractor space expands. Recency and scale both help, neither suffices. Latest-generation and larger models lead within their families. But, gpt-oss-120b and granite-3-3-8b (above several frontier-prior models at 8B) show that training-data composition and instruction tuning contribute alongside scale and recency. Accuracy ranking diverges from operational impact. We estimate operational impact by summing expert-authored per-rule savings over each models correctly answered questions. As shown in Appendix A.8, the savings ranking diverges from the accuracy ranking for several models: gemini3.1-pro-pre leads on savings (USD 3.134.82M) while claude-opus-4-6, the accuracy leader, is second. This divergence implies that model 12 points lower on accuracy but consistently correct on the highest-impact rules may be the better deployment choice. Frontier progression has stalled on industrialmaintenance reasoning. As shown in Figure 5, the GPQA Diamond [31] leadership band has advanced 19.4 points (o1 74.7% Gemini3.1-Pro-Preview 94.1%) while the DiagnosticIQ leadership band has advanced only 8.2 points (o1 65.4% Claude-Opus-4.6 73.6%). The two bands now sit 20.5 points apart, and the gap has widened over time. Moreover, Anthropics Sonnet 4.6 advanced on GPQA Diamond (79.9%) yet regressed 5.77 points on DiagnosticIQ below its Feb 2025 predecessor, suggesting that capability gains on general-science benchmarks do not always transfer. We attribute this to frontier post-training optimizing heavily for generalscience reasoning, where gains do not transfer cleanly to industrial-maintenance reasoning. Figure 5: Field-wide leadership on GPQA Diamond [2], DiagnosticIQ and DiagnosticIQ Pro

sections.title:
5.2 Detailed Analysis
Beyond aggregate accuracy, we ask how models
fail and which questions are hard. This section predominantly uses the dataset variants introduced in
Section 4.1.
sections.content: Models reason competently within templates. We conducted an expert study with five experts, who rated mistral-large rationales on 27 questions sampled from DiagnosticIQ Rationale. We find that 85% of paired annotations reached consensus at the favorable threshold, with quadratic-weighted Cohens Kappa of 0.220.28 across pairs (moderate agreement; full breakdown in Appendix A.9.2). Inter-rater agreement was lowest for assets requiring fine-grained operational knowledge (Plate & Frame heat exchangers). Overall, even mid-tier models produce explanations consistent with domain reasoning on our prepared dataset. This sets up the central question: what happens when the rule structure is preserved but the logical content is altered? Yet models pattern-match more than reason about conditions. The DiagnosticIQ Aug variant probes this directly. After temporal-operator inversion, the original action is no longer logically supported, so reasoning-grounded model should select None of the above. Across the three models we evaluated on DiagnosticIQ Aug, only 15.516.6% do so, while half to two-thirds (48.863.4%) persist with the original answer. Strikingly, claude-opus-4-6, the leaderboard leader on DiagnosticIQ, shows the strongest logical inertia (63.4%), suggesting more capable models are more confidently wrong on this probe rather than more careful. complementary per-rule analysis (Appendix D) corroborates this: accuracy is sharply bimodal across the 118 rules, the signature of rule-shape recognition. Current LLMs treat structured industrial rules more as templates to recognize than as conditions to evaluate. See Appendix A.9.1 for details. Models lose accuracy under paraphrasing. The pattern repeats when we paraphrase symbolic conditions into prose (DiagnosticIQ Verbose). Across 17 models, paraphrasing reduces Macro accuracy by 4.2 points on average (range 1.8 to 9.6). The drop indicates that current LLMs in this domain rely on symbolic structure as reasoning scaffolding, and paraphrasing erodes that structure. Per-model breakdowns are available in Appendix A.12. 8 Table 3: Left: PertEval robustness on DiagnosticIQ Pert. ** α=0.01, * α=0.05. Right: Cross-asset generalization experiments (AHU Other).

Across the 16 asset types, claude-opus-4-6 exceeds 85% on UPS, Plate & Frame, Boiler, and HXU, but drops below 35% on Pump and Cooling Tower under the Pro variant, with an average Pro drop of 13.8 points. The largest Pro drops occur on assets whose rules span interrelated subsystems. Question type matters as well: elimination questions consistently yield higher accuracy than selection (mistral-large: 71.0% vs. 63.9% on DiagnosticIQ), and the selection gap widens sharply on DiagnosticIQ Pro. Per-asset and per-question-type breakdowns are in Appendix A.11 and Appendix Table 10 respectively. Performance drops under prompt-level perturbation. robustness analysis using DiagnosticIQ Pert reaches the same conclusion through different route: no model achieves more than 51.5% consistency across original and perturbed phrasings, and the best frontier model lands at 48% (See Table 3). Among frontier models, claude-opus-4-6 and gemini-3.1-pro-pre have negligible PDRs (0.01), while gpt-5.4 shows the largest significant drop (0.05). We can see that leading LLMs are correct on roughly only half the questions when robustness across phrasing is required. Difficulty predictors within DiagnosticIQ. We build logistic regression model to predict per-question correctness for claude-opus-4-6 using four features (Figure 6). The strongest predictor of failure is special-function count: each additional arithmetic or comparison operation cuts the odds of correctness by 47% (β=0.627, eβ=0.53, p<0.001). Counterintuitively, logical complexity (number of clauses in the condition tree) helps accuracy (β= + 0.851, p<0.001), plausibly because longer boolean expressions give the model more structural scaffolding rather than additional load to track. separate ablation reinforces this scaffolding interpretation: presenting the full condition tree rather than DNF-expanded conjunction improves accuracy by 0.941.60 percentage points across three models (Appendix A.13). Prompt length has modest positive effect (β= + 0.122); option count is not significant (p=0.449), suggesting that Pro-variant difficulty arises from distractor quality rather than option count alone. Together, these results challenge the common framing that compositional logic is uniformly harder for LLMs: structure helps and arithmetic hurts. Full coefficients in Appendix A.10. Figure 6: Mean feature values for correctly answered, failed, and bottom-10% questions.

sections.title: 5.3 Cross-Asset/Rule Generalization
sections.content: We partition DiagnosticIQ into AHU rules (the dominant class, 58%) and Other (all remaining asset types). We fine-tune three 8B-scale base models on the AHU split using two methods, supervised fine-tuning on rationales from DiagnosticIQ Rationale and GRPO technique on the correctness signal, and evaluate on Other. Both methods improve over the base model, but the optimal choice depends on whether the base model is reasoning-tuned (Table 3 : Right Side). For non-reasoning bases (Llama-3.1, Granite-3.3), GRPO substantially outperforms SFT and improves cross-asset generalization (Llama: +12.84 Macro; Granite: +3.6). For the reasoning-tuned base (Qwen3), the pattern reverses: SFT yields +10.5 Macro gain while GRPO underperforms it. This bifurcation suggests that supervised distillation works best when the base model already exposes structured 9 reasoning traces, while preference-based optimization is more effective when starting from model without reasoning structure. Moreover, on 11 held-out SME-authored rules, one-shot prompting with DiagnosticIQ exemplars improves MAP@1 from 41.7% to 58.3% (Appendix A.14).

sections.title: 6 Conclusion and Limitations
sections.content: Our DiagnosticIQ benchmark and its variants reveal consistent brittleness arc: the leading model scores 73.6% Macro, 59.8% on Pro, and 48% consistency on Pert. Paraphrasing and operatorinversion experiments show that current LLMs treat structured rules as templates rather than conditions to evaluate. This 48% robustness floor sits near frontier accuracy on Humanitys Last Exam [27] (46%, May 2026), suggesting that gains on general-knowledge benchmarks overstate readiness for high-stakes operational deployment where consistency across phrasings is non-negotiable. Limitations: Our scope covers English-language rules under zero-shot evaluation, leaving multilingual rules, agentic settings, and the full 800+ industrial asset taxonomy as future work.

sections.title: A Technical appendices and supplementary material
sections.content: The appendix is organized into four parts: Dataset construction and background (Sections A.1A.6): asset types, rule provenance, MCQA pipeline details, hyperparameter analysis, and per-variant construction notes. Supplementary analyses (Sections A.7A.15): full results for ablations and probes referenced from the main body. Extended empirical analyses (Section B): per-asset accuracy heatmap, selection vs elimination gap, universal-failure questions, and Bradley-Terry Elo ratings. Human evaluation (Section C): study design, per-respondent and per-asset results, universalfailure question listing, and limitations. Reproducibility, declarations, and ethics (Sections A.16A.18). A.1 Asset Type Descriptions The DiagnosticIQ dataset includes diagnostic rules derived from wide range of industrial asset types commonly found in commercial buildings, data centers, manufacturing facilities, and other operational environments. Each asset class is associated with domain-specific behaviors, sensor signals, and potential fault conditions that inform the construction of diagnostic questions. Table 4 describes the primary asset types represented in the dataset. Table 4: Industrial asset types addressed by the diagnostic rules in DiagnosticIQ. Asset Type Description AHU (Air Handling Unit) Air Compressor Boiler Chiller Condenser Cooling Tower CRAC (Computer Room AC) ERV (Energy Recovery Ventilator) Fan Heat Exchanger Plate & Frame HX Pump Terminal Unit UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) VAV (Variable Air Volume Unit) Water Heater Conditions and circulates air as part of an HVAC system. Regulates airflow, temperature, and humidity in commercial buildings. Converts electrical or mechanical power into pressurized air for pneumatic systems and equipment. Heats water or other fluids for use in heating systems, industrial processes, or power generation. Removes heat from water using vapor-compression or absorption cycles. Supplies chilled water to cooling systems. Rejects heat from refrigerant cycles in chillers or heat pumps. Rejects heat from water-cooled systems by dissipating it into the atmosphere. Common in HVAC and process cooling. Cools air in data centers to maintain safe temperature and humidity for IT equipment. Transfers heat and moisture between exhaust and incoming fresh air streams. Drives air movement for ventilation, circulation, or cooling. Transfers heat between two fluids without mixing. Used for efficient thermal regulation in building systems. Compact heat exchanger using stacked plates to transfer heat between fluid streams. Moves liquids through mechanical force. Used in chilled water, hot water, and process fluid systems. Regulates temperature and air delivery in individual building zones. Provides temporary backup power during grid interruptions. Controls airflow to zone by varying damper position. Provides domestic or process hot water. May be gas or electric. A.2 Rule Provenance and Curation Figure 7 provides an outline of how expert rules were constructed. The rules originated from the Smarter Buildings initiative (introduced 2011 as part of the larger Smarter Planet program [41]), which began with 18 Fault Detection and Diagnostic (FDD) rules across 3 equipment types, focused Figure 7: The five-stage rule construction lifecycle underlying DiagnosticIQ. Reliability Engineers, System Administrators, and Rules Logic committee author rules using ASHRAE guidelines (1), which trigger sensor-driven alarms (2) and prompt engineer actions (3). Savings are monitored over six months (4) and feed back into rule refinement. DiagnosticIQ (5) takes alarms/rules from Stage 2 as input and outputs recommended actions, with energy savings as the deployment metric. primarily on Air Handlers. The program targeted 515% energy savings on monitored equipment and 30% reduction in maintenance hours. Over seven years of operational deployment with 11 major updates, the corpus grew to 118 active rules (deployed in production system [35]) across 16 equipment types. Table 5 reports the per-asset breakdown of rules, disjunctions, atomic conditions, and observations. team of 10 domain experts contributed to rule development across the seven-year program. Two roles drove the core work: Reliability Engineer who developed the rules and System Administrator who coded them. Rules Logic committee of 28 participants met biweekly to brainstorm and refine the logic. Across the full team, members held 1015 years of operational experience maintaining and managing physical infrastructure assets, carried industry certifications in their respective specializations (e.g., Certified Facility Manager (CFM), Certified Energy Manager (CEM), Certified Maintenance and Reliability Professional (CMRP), Professional Engineer (PE)), and had collectively deployed fault-detection systems across three continents (North America, South America, and Europe). New rules typically took 30 minutes to code but considerably longer to test, document, and finalize, including authoring technician-facing verbiage about potential causes and troubleshooting steps. Table 5: Statistics of expert-curated rules in DiagnosticIQ. Columns: number of rules per asset, number of disjunctions (), number of atomic conditions (C), and number of unique observations referenced. Asset descriptions in Table 4. Unique observations are 193. Asset Type # Rules # Disjunctions # Atomic Cond. # Observations AHU Chiller CRAC VAV Boiler Pump Cooling Tower HXU Air Compressor Meter PDU PIU Fan Lighting Plate & Frame UPS 55 11 10 8 6 5 4 4 3 3 3 2 1 1 1 1 Total 118 172 27 28 18 19 22 12 9 8 4 3 5 4 2 4 1 340 312 26 21 30 17 20 11 12 6 4 7 5 4 2 4 2 485 54 0 0 3 5 1 1 0 1 4 0 1 0 1 1 72 16 Figure 8: Word count distribution of unique actions in the expert-curated dataset. A.3 Rule Categorization and Examples We categorize the rule corpus by the Boolean logic structure of the condition tree: Conjunctive (CNF). All conditions must be simultaneously satisfied. Example: CRAC Running AND (Return Temp Supply Temp + 3F). Disjunctive (OR). Any condition is sufficient to trigger the rule. Example: ABS[Pressure Setpoint] > 10 PSI OR Pressure > 130 PSI. Mixed (AND-OR). Structured combinations of conjunctive and disjunctive logic, typically in disjunctive normal form. Example: AHU Running AND (OAT SAT > 5F OR SAT Not Reporting) AND (Heating Valve > 10% OR Preheat Valve > 10%). Negation-based. Includes explicitly negated conditions. Example: (Parent AHU NOT Running) AND (SubType NOT FCU) AND (Supply Air Flow > 80 CFM) AND (Requirements met for 2 Hours). This categorization aids interpretability and supports systematic debugging of rule interactions in complex monitoring systems. A.4 MCQA Generation Pipeline Details Algorithm 1 describes the question-generation procedure. The pipeline takes as input the expertauthored rule corpus {Ri}, asset descriptions Desc, and parameter max_n_choices controlling the option count per question. For each rule Ri, the procedure executes four steps: 1. Condition extraction. Atomic conditions {QC k} are extracted from the rules condition tree Ri. 2. Asset retrieval. The matching asset description ADi is retrieved from Desc. 3. Option generation. Candidate (question, options, answer) tuples are produced via two strategies: selection (relevant observations RRSim-similar to the rule action) and elimination (irrelevant observations as distractors). 4. Question assembly. Each extracted condition is combined with each (question, options, answer) tuple to produce one MCQA item, which is appended to the dataset DSQ. An example generated prompt is shown in Figure 15. Pipeline extensibility. To demonstrate that the MCQA-generation pipeline extends beyond our primary rule corpus, we applied it to 28 rules manually extracted from related AHU-focused 17 Algorithm 1 QA Generation Pipeline for DiagnosticIQ Require: Rule corpus {R1, . . . , RNR}; asset descriptions Desc; relevance-similarity model RRSim; observation pool O; hyperparameters α, β, max_n_choices Ensure: Questionanswer dataset DSQ 1: DSQ [ ] 2: for each rule Ri {R1, . . . , RNR } do k}N cond k=1 EXTRACTCONDITIONS(Ri) {QC ADi GETASSETDESCRIPTION(Ri, Desc) question conditions per rule asset description string sel EXTRACTOBSSELECTION(Ri, α, RRSim, O) i sel tuples (QP, OP T, A) via selection mode eli EXTRACTOBSELIMINATION(Ri, β, RRSim, O) mode all_optsi sel eli 3: 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: eli tuples via elimination union of both option pools single MCQA item for each condition QC k=1 do for each (QP, OP T, A) all_optsi do k, QP, OP T, A) {QC (ADi, QC DSQ.APPEND(Q) cond k}N 9: 10: 11: 12: 13: 14: end for 15: return DSQ end for end for work [18], generating approximately 400 additional MCQA items. The extension is released as supplementary dataset, evidencing that both the construction methodology and the resulting rules generalize to independently authored rule sources. A.5 Hyperparameter Selection We analyze the effect of varying α and β (the per-rule selection and elimination distractor counts) on dataset option diversity. Larger values produce more questions but at the cost of higher option overlap across questions derived from the same rule. We quantify diversity using the question-to-question Intersection over Union (IoU) of generated options, computed across questions from rules with ids {1, 4, 9, 16, 25}: IoUmean = 1 DQ (cid:88) x1,x2DQ OP Tx1 OP Tx2 OP Tx1 OP Tx2 Figure 9 shows that increasing α, β grows dataset size but also raises mean IoU (lower diversity). We choose α = β = 10 as pragmatic balance on the size-vs-diversity Pareto frontier: smaller values yield higher diversity but reduce per-rule question counts below the threshold needed for stable accuracy estimates (45 questions per rule at α = β = 10), while larger values inflate dataset size with diminishing diversity returns. A.5.1 Rule-to-Rule Similarity Map We compute Rule-to-Rule Similarity (RRSim) by embedding the textual representation of each rules condition tree (asset_type and conditions fields) using all-mpnet-base-v2 and computing cosine similarity between embeddings. RRSim drives distractor selection at question-generation time: selection questions sample distractors from semantically dissimilar rules (operationally adjacent but distinct), while elimination questions invert this polarity. A.6 DiagnosticIQ Variants This subsection details the construction procedure for each released variant. 18 Figure 9: Dataset size vs. mean IoU as α and β vary. Bubble size is proportional to question count; higher α, β yields more questions but lower option diversity. Figure 10: Rule-to-rule similarity heatmap based on all-mpnet-base-v2 embeddings of rule conditions. A.6.1 DiagnosticIQ Pro DiagnosticIQ Pro extends DiagnosticIQ to evaluate model performance with larger answer sets. For each question Qi in DiagnosticIQ, we retain the asset description ADi, observed conditions QC i, prompt QP i, and ground-truth answer Ai, while expanding the option set OP from 4 to 10 by adding plausible distractors. For selection questions, additional distractors are sampled from observations of semantically similar but distinct rules. For elimination questions, both correct and incorrect option sets are augmented using RRSim to maintain logical coherence. The expanded variant mimics real-world scenarios where practitioners must consider many potential failure causes and tests model robustness in high-option environments. 19 A.6.2 DiagnosticIQ Pert DiagnosticIQ Pert evaluates response sensitivity to surface-level perturbations. We apply four transformations to each DiagnosticIQ question: random shuffling of conditions and options, parenthesizing option labels (e.g., (A)), changing label characters (e.g., A, B, P, Q, R), and substituting question prompts QP with alternates from the template pool. These perturbations preserve semantic content while varying surface form. A.6.3 DiagnosticIQ Verbose DiagnosticIQ Verbose paraphrases the symbolic conditions of each question into natural language. We embed conditions of each question using all-mpnet-base-v2 and cluster via cosine similarity to identify 10 representative questions across the rule corpus. We manually convert these representative cases to natural language, then prompt mistral-large with these as in-context examples to generate paraphrases for the remaining questions (Figure 16). A.6.4 DiagnosticIQ-Aug DiagnosticIQ-Aug applies logical inversion to each rule: all temporal comparison operators are inverted (<>, ), and None of the above is added as new answer option. After inversion, the original action is no longer logically supported, so reasoning-grounded model should select None of the above. Construction details and the full probe analysis are in Section A.9. A.7 Asset Description in the Prompt Motivation. Industrial assets of the same type can differ substantially in configuration. To anchor questions in this context, we include concise asset description (AD) in every prompt, written by subject matter experts  (Table 4)  . The full prompt template is shown in Figure 15. Setup. We measure how much AD contributes by removing it from the base template while keeping all other fields identical. Three models spanning the capability spectrum are re-evaluated. Result. Removing AD reduces Macro Accuracy by 8.610.2 points across all three models  (Table 6)  ; even the strongest model (claude-opus-4-6) loses 8.77 points without asset context. This confirms AD carries load-bearing context, not presentational decoration. The result also suggests prompt engineering itself is meaningful axis for future research; we leave systematic prompt optimization (e.g., GEPA [1]) as future work. Table 6: Effect of removing the asset description (AD) from the prompt. Performance is the change in Macro Accuracy on DiagnosticIQ relative to the full prompt.

Each rule in DiagnosticIQ carries an associated cost-savings range (Min, Max) authored by the same domain experts who developed the rules over seven years of deployment, capturing the energy and downtime savings observed when correct actions were taken on triggered conditions. Estimation procedure. For each LLM, we sum the cost-savings ranges of every question the model answered correctly on DiagnosticIQ. Incorrect answers, whether false positives or false negatives, are conservatively assigned zero savings. This is deliberately strict accounting: wrong action may still incidentally save some energy in practice, but quantifying suboptimal-action savings would require infeasible combinatorial analysis. The zero-savings convention treats only verified-correct recommendations as value-generating. 20 Result. Table 7 reports estimated total savings for the top five models alongside Micro accuracy on DiagnosticIQ. Two findings emerge. First, the leading model contributes USD 3.134.82M in estimated savings, demonstrating LLM-based action recommendation is operationally meaningful. Second, ranking by savings differs from ranking by accuracy: gemini-3.1-pro-pre achieves the highest savings despite ranking second on Micro accuracy (75.92% vs. claude-opus-4-6s 77.76%), because it answers correctly on higher proportion of high-savings rules. Conversely, claude-3-7-sonnet (prior-generation accuracy leader at 72.66%) ranks fifth on savings, indicating its correct answers concentrate on lower-impact rules. Table 7: Estimated cost savings on DiagnosticIQ from correct LLM recommendations alongside Micro accuracy. Savings are summed over correctly answered rules; incorrect answers are assigned zero savings.

The savings-vs-accuracy decoupling has two practical consequences. For benchmark design, future iterations of DiagnosticIQ could weight Macro accuracy by per-rule impact, producing an impact-weighted accuracy metric. For deployment, organizations selecting models for industrial maintenance should consider impact-weighted evaluation alongside raw accuracy. We release per-rule savings annotations alongside the dataset. A.9 Reasoning vs. Pattern Matching: Full Probes natural concern with DiagnosticIQ is that, because all questions are generated from 120 source rules, models may achieve high accuracy by recognizing rule structure rather than reasoning about conditions. We design two complementary probes. A.9.1 Logical inversion via condition flipping. For each rule in DiagnosticIQ, we construct perturbed variant in which all temporal comparison operators are inverted (<>, ), reversing the rules truth condition. To this perturbed dataset, we add None of the above as new answer option. After inversion, the rule no longer fires under the original conditions; the original correct action is no longer supported, and the only logically valid answer is None of the above. reasoning-grounded model should select the new option; pattern-matching model will select the original answer despite the inversion. Result. Table 8 reports answer distributions for three models. Across all three, the dominant choice is the previously correct answer, even though that answer is now logically inconsistent with the inverted conditions. Claude-Opus-4-6 selects the original answer 63% of the time and None of the above only 21% of the time. Mistral-Medium and Mistral-Small show the same systematic failure mode (50% and 49% old-answer selection). The old action dominates by 3 to 4 margin. Table 8: Answer distribution on DiagnosticIQ-Aug, where temporal operators are inverted. None of the above is the only logically valid answer.

As complementary check, we collect chain-of-thought rationales from mistral-large on the unperturbed DiagnosticIQ and have five domain experts rate them along two axes: knowledge accuracy (does the explanation cite the right operational principles?) and operational relevance (does the explanation match how technician would reason about the alarm?). Experts rated 27 representative rationales on 010 Likert scale; example rationales annotated with expert comments are in Figures 12 and 13. Figure 11 reports the rating distribution. Despite the condition-flipping failure exposed in Probe 1, expert ratings on unperturbed inputs are predominantly favorable: the models reasoning generally aligns with expert judgment, supporting its potential to augment maintenance tasks. However, for certain assets (PDU, Pump, Boiler), inter-rater disagreement is high, with expert feedback indicating differing expectations regarding the required granularity of explanations. The model consistently scored lower on Plate & Frame HX assets; experts noted the rationales lacked nuanced operational knowledge, signifying domain-specific knowledge gap. cumulative agreement analysis shows consensus is high at threshold 8 (85% of annotators, 75% pairwise) but decreases at stricter thresholds. Pairwise quadratic weighted Kappa between annotators ranges from 0.22 to 0.28 (moderate agreement); the spread is largely attributable to single outlier annotator, with the remaining experts demonstrating substantially closer agreement. Figure 11: Expert ratings of model-generated rationales for mistral-large. What the two probes together tell us. Models can produce expert-acceptable reasoning when the rule structure aligns with the underlying logic, but they fail to detect when that structure has been logically inverted. The condition-flipping result is the kind of finding that benchmark of this design enables: by holding rule structure constant and varying only the logical content, we isolate logical sensitivity from surface pattern recognition. The persistent old-answer selection rate across capability tiers is quantifiable failure mode that future work can target directly. Annotator agreement and disagreement examples. Figure 12 shows case where annotators disagreed with the LLM-generated rationale, with the experts reasoning for low rating. Figure 13 shows case where annotators agreed. A.10 What Makes Question Hard? Difficulty Decomposition We ask which structural properties of question predict whether an LLM will answer it correctly. This decomposition characterizes the kinds of reasoning current LLMs handle well or poorly and helps benchmark consumers identify which subsets of DiagnosticIQ pose the greatest difficulty. Setup. We extract four features from every question in DiagnosticIQ core (n = 6,690): Logic complexity index: number of logical symbols (AND, OR, NOT) in the rule. Special function count: number of mathematical or comparison operations (e.g., SUM, AVG, IF, <, >, =). Length of question text: total prompt character count, normalized. Number of options: 4 in DiagnosticIQ core; included as confounding control. We fit logistic regression to predict per-question correctness for the leaderboard-leading model (claude-opus-4-6, accuracy 73.59%). Significance is assessed via Wald z-test. 22 Figure 12: Rationale generated where annotator disagreed. ### Asset Description: Pump: device used to move fluid or gas from one place to another. It can include various types of pumps, such as centrifugal pumps, positive displacement pumps, and diaphragm pumps. ### Conditions: - Pump Running - SubType NOT HW - Pump Motor VFD Output % > 35% - Pump Motor VFD Output % = Daily Average Pump Motor VFD Output % - OAT < 95 - ABS(Pump Differential Pressure - Pump Differential Pressure Setpoint) > 4psi IF Reporting ### How long the conditions were met: Met for 2 Hours Analyse the given conditions of the presented asset and select the option that MOST likely gives the reason for the conditions? A. Lighting relay schedule over written B. Mis-sized equipment C. Unit bypassing or blowing off too much air D. Static pressure sensors need calibration, repair or replacement # Answer Answer: D. Static pressure sensors need calibration, repair or replacement Guidance Rationale: Given that the pump is running and the motor's variable frequency drive (VFD) output percentage is both above 35% and equal to the daily average, this indicates that the pump is operating under stable and expected load conditions. However, there is significant deviation in the pump differential pressure from its setpoint, exceeding 4 psi, which points to potential issue in how pressure is being measured. Since the outside air temperature is below 95F, it is unlikely that environmental conditions are responsible for the discrepancy. Therefore, the most likely explanation is that the static pressure sensors require calibration, repair, or replacement, to ensure accurate readings and efficient pump operation. Expert Rating: 2 Expert Comment: This rule is looking for VFD that should be changing speed but is not. The VFD or control system may be in manual mode or there is control system failure. The reference to positive displacement pumps and diaphragm pumps does not make sense in the context of this rule. Result. Three of four features are highly significant (p < 0.001); see Table 9. First, special-function count is the strongest predictor of failure: each additional arithmetic or comparison operation reduces the odds of correctness by approximately 47% (odds ratio e0.627 0.53). Rules dense with arithmetic and threshold comparisons, common in HVAC and chiller diagnostics, are systematically harder than rules dominated by simple state predicates. Second, and counterintuitively, logical complexity helps: each additional AND/OR connective increases the odds of correctness (odds ratio 2.34). One plausible explanation: longer Boolean expressions provide structural scaffolding that helps anchor the models reasoning, whereas terse rules with few connectives leave more room for misinterpretation. This flips common assumption that compositional logic is universally harder. Question text length has small positive effect (+0.122, = 0.002); option count is not significant (p = 0.449), confirming Pro-variant difficulty stems from set-size brittleness, not per-question complexity. Implications. For benchmark interpretation, aggregate accuracy can obscure structural failure modes: model scoring 73% may be near-perfect on simple rules and barely above chance on 23 Figure 13: Rationale generated where annotator agreed. ### Asset Description: Chiller: device that removes heat from liquid through vapor-compression or vapor-absorption refrigeration process. ### Conditions: - Run Status = 0 - Power Input > 5 KW ### How long the conditions were met: Met for 3 Hours What underlying factor could explain the current asset state? A. Excessive Cooling B. Could be oil heater C. Filters clogged D. Excess equipment is on # Answer Answer: B. Could be oil heater Guidance Rationale: Given that the chiller's run status is 0, it is not actively performing its primary cooling function. However, the system is still consuming more than 5 kW of power for continuous duration of 3 hours, indicating non-trivial energy usage during downtime. This suggests that the power draw is likely attributed to an auxiliary component, rather than the main compressor or cooling cycle. common explanation for such behavior is the operation of an oil heater, which is designed to maintain the proper viscosity of lubricants or ensure safe startup during idle periods. Expert Rating: 10 Expert Comment: liked that it also (maybe unintentionally) hinted at the fact that it could be because of an auxiliary component being on. We provide per-question feature annotations in the dataset release. A.11 Per-Asset and Per-Question-Type Analysis Asset-wise analysis. Figure 14 reports per-asset accuracy for claude-opus-4-6, the leading model. Performance varies substantially: UPS, Plate & Frame, Boiler, and HXU exceed 85% on DiagnosticIQ, while CRAC, Meter, Cooling Tower, and PDU sit below 65%. The Pro variant compounds these gaps unevenly: the average drop is 13.8 points, but Pump (27.4), HXU (25.0), and Cooling Tower (24.5) lose the most ground, while Meter (1.7), Boiler (5.9), and UPS (5.0) remain stable. 24 The largest drops appear on assets whose rules span interrelated subsystems (cooling towers depend on chiller and ambient state; pumps on flow and pressure across multiple sensors), suggesting Pro penalizes structurally complex assets disproportionately. PDU is an exception with identical accuracy (60.0%) on both variants, plausibly because its rules are short and the failure mode is consistent across distractor counts. Figure 14: Per-asset accuracy for claude-opus-4-6 on DiagnosticIQ and DiagnosticIQ Pro. Selection vs. elimination. Elimination questions yield consistently higher accuracy than selection across models (mistral-large: 71.0% vs. 63.9% on DiagnosticIQ; Table 10). The gap widens on DiagnosticIQ Pro: mistral-large drops 35.0 points on selection (63.9% 28.9%) but only 8.4 on elimination (71.0% 62.6%). The pattern replicates across model families, suggesting that ranking the most relevant action from large adjacent pool is harder than identifying which action is unsupported by the rule. A.13 Ablation: Condition Tree vs. Conjunctive Scenarios Our pipeline converts each rule into Disjunctive Normal Form and presents one conjunctive clause per question. natural alternative is to retain the original condition tree, preserving the full disjunctive structure in single prompt. We compare the two presentations on held-out subset of DiagnosticIQ for three models. Result. Condition-tree presentation yields small but consistent accuracy gain across all three models  (Table 12)  : claude-opus-4-6 gains +1.60 points, mistral-medium +1.20, mistral-small +0.94. The direction is expected (additional disjunctive context provides more signal), but the magnitude is modest, well below the 4.3-point symbolic-to-linguistic gap and the 1314 point drop from DiagnosticIQ to DiagnosticIQ Pro reported in the main paper. First, operational fidelity: rules in the production deployment underlying DiagnosticIQ execute as conjunctions, one fired clause at time, so evaluating 26 on conjunctions matches the runtime input format. Second, rule-explosion mitigation: condition trees with many disjunctions produce combinatorially many trigger scenarios per rule, and DNF expansion makes each independently probeable. Both presentation variants are released alongside the dataset. A.14 Few-Shot Generalization to Unseen Rules To test whether models can generalize the rule-to-action mapping to entirely new rules, we collect 11 SME-authored rules with ground-truth actions held out from DiagnosticIQ. For each new rule, we sample 10 candidate actions using all-MiniLM-L6-v2 to construct realistic distractors, and measure MAP@1 (whether the ground-truth action is the top-1 recommendation). We provide mistral-large with 05 in-context examples drawn from DiagnosticIQ. Result. In-context examples improve MAP@1 over zero-shot, but the gain is non-monotonic: 41.67% (0-shot), 58.33% (1-shot), 50.00% (2-shot), 50.00% (3-shot), 50.00% (4-shot), 50.00% (5-shot). 1-shot is strongest, suggesting single example is sufficient to anchor the model on the rule-toaction format; additional demonstrations neither help nor hurt, indicating diminishing returns. We caveat these results with the small sample size (11 rules); larger held-out corpus would give firmer conclusions. In-context examples MAP@1 (%) 0-shot 1-shot 2-shot 3-shot 4-shot 5-shot 41.67 58.33 50.00 50.00 50.00 50.00 A.15 Set-Size Error Severity Analysis To analyze the nature of model errors, we compute semantic rank for each incorrect prediction and set-size measure of error severity. Semantic rank. Each answer choice is embedded into semantic vector space using pretrained embedding model. For every wrong prediction, we measure its distance to the correct option and rank all options from closest to farthest. semantic rank of 1 indicates the model selected the wrong option most semantically similar to the correct answer (perfect semantic confusion); higher ranks indicate progressively less semantically related choices. Table 14 reports mean ranks for selected models on DiagnosticIQ and DiagnosticIQ Pert. Set-size error severity. For each model we compute the average size of the predicted answer set across all questions (Avg_Set_All) and across only incorrectly answered questions (Avg_Set_Wrong). Tables 15 and 16 report these for DiagnosticIQ and DiagnosticIQ Pro respectively. Across both datasets, models consistently exhibit higher average set sizes on incorrectly answered items than on the overall set, indicating systematic patterns in error severity. Two-sample t-tests and MannWhitney tests yield extremely small p-values for nearly all models, confirming the score distributions for correct and incorrect items differ significantly. Models not only struggle more with difficult cases but also produce substantially larger deviations when they fail, reflecting meaningful gap in calibration under challenging conditions. Options: (P) Control system sent the wrong command (Q) Belts are loose or broken (S) Broken Belt (R) Vanes at wrong angle Your output must strictly follow this format: {"answer": <the list of selected options, e.g., ["(P)", "(R)"]>} A.17 Generative AI Usage Declaration During the preparation of this work, the authors used Grammarly to improve grammar, clarity, and flow of the manuscript. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication. A.18 Ethics Statement We strictly obey the principles outlined in the NeurIPS Code of Ethics, including careful consideration of potential ethical concerns regarding human subjects, data privacy, and fairness in dataset construction decisions. All data used in this study were released in compliance with legal and ethical standards, with proper security measures to safeguard personal and location information. The dataset hosting platform will be Hugging Face and/or Kaggle. The human evaluation was voluntary multiple-choice survey of 9 adult engineering practitioners drawn from our research network. Participation was anonymous from the standpoint of published results: respondents are referred to only as R1R9, and self-reported experience descriptions are aggregated (Appendix C). No personally identifiable information appears in the dataset or paper. No clinical, educational, financial, or employment decisions were made on the basis of any respondents answers. Respondents were informed that their responses would be reported in aggregate in an academic publication, and submission constituted consent. The study presented no foreseeable risks beyond those of routine professional discussion. 30 Figure 16: Prompt used to convert symbolic representation to natural language. Your task is to read the asset description (## Asset Description:) and conditions (## Conditions:) applied on the asset and write the conditions in natural language. Several examples are provided; complete the last sample. ## Conditions: AHU Running OAT < 80 Fahrenheit Cooling Valve % > 97% ABS(Supply Air Temperature Setpoint - Supply Air Temperature) > 3 IF Setpoint Reporting ## Conditions in Natural Language: The asset is running while the outside temperature is less than 80 Fahrenheit and the unit's cooling valve is nearly fully open (Cooling Valve is open more than 97%) ... ## Asset Description: AHU: Air Handling Unit: device used to condition and circulate air as part of heating, ventilating, and air-conditioning (HVAC) system. ## Conditions: AHU Running OAT > 35 Fahrenheit Preheat Valve % > 97% ## Conditions in Natural Language:

sections.title: B Extended Empirical Analyses
sections.content: This appendix presents four analyses derived from per-question evaluation results across all 29 LLMs on DiagnosticIQ simpleV. B.1 Universal-Failure Questions We define universal-failure question as one that no LLM in our 29-model evaluation answered correctly. These questions are diagnostically interesting because they reveal failure modes shared across the full LLM population, not specific to one family or scale. Figure 17 summarizes the universal-failure population. Panel (A) shows the full distribution of question difficulty. Panel (B) shows per-asset universal-failure rate. Universal failures are 2.8% of the benchmark. Of 5,180 simpleV positive-type questions, 144 are universal failures (2.8%) and 178 are universal passes (3.4%). The bulk of questions sit between these extremes. The mid-range distribution is roughly U-shaped, with small bumps at both ends and heavier population on the easy side (more questions where 20+ models succeed than where 5 or fewer succeed). CRAC dominates the universal-failure population. Per-asset universal-failure rate (Figure 17B) ranges from 0% (UPS, HXU, Plate & Frame) to 11.4% (CRAC). CRAC questions account for 32 of 144 total universal failures despite representing only 280 of 5,180 questions in the benchmark. Three other asset classes show elevated rates: PDU (6.7%), Air Compressor (5.5%), and Lighting/Fan (5.0%). The remaining classes sit below 5%. What makes CRAC questions hard for all models. We inspected sample of CRAC universalfailure questions. Three patterns recur. First, CRAC rules frequently involve nested temporal conditions (temperature exceeded threshold for 3 hours after maintenance flag cleared) that require tracking multiple stateful variables. Second, the correct diagnoses often involve compound causal chains (sensor fault upstream caused compressor cycling) rather than single-pattern matches. Third, the distractor options for CRAC questions tend to be syntactically similar to the correct answer, making lexical-match heuristics unhelpful. Together these patterns suggest CRAC is the asset class 31 Figure 17: Question difficulty across 5,180 positive-type simpleV questions. (A) Histogram of n_correct values. Bars colored from red (0 correct) to green (29 correct). 144 questions are universal failures (0 of 29 correct); 178 are universal passes (all 29 correct). (B) Per-asset universal-failure rate. CRAC stands out at 11.4% universal-failure rate, more than 1.7 the next-highest asset class. most demanding of genuine multi-step diagnostic reasoning, and that current LLMs are uniformly weak at this style of inference. Universal failures map to small set of rules. Of the 144 universal-failure questions, the majority concentrate on small set of rules. We provide the full universal-failure question list as supplementary material (universal_failures_full.csv). Researchers seeking targeted training data for industrial diagnostic reasoning may find these questions useful starting point. B.2 Wilson 95% Confidence Intervals on the Main Leaderboard We compute Wilson 95% confidence intervals for every cell of the main leaderboard  (Table 2)  from the existing per-question correctness data. No model re-evaluation is required. Method. Wilson score intervals are the recommended interval for binomial proportions and have better coverage near 0 and 1 than Wald intervals [5]. Micro accuracy is single Bernoulli proportion (correct over total questions); its CI is direct Wilson. Macro accuracy is the mean of per-asset Bernoulli proportions; we compute each per-asset CI via Wilson, then propagate to Macro CI via the delta method, treating per-asset accuracies as independent. The delta-method CI is normalapproximation in form but uses the per-asset Wilson variances rather than Wald variances. Result. Table 17 reports per-cell CIs. Two findings are notable. Top-3 Macro CIs overlap. On DiagnosticIQ, claude-opus-4-6 (73.59 2.21), gpt-5.4 (73.27 2.01), and gemini-3.1-pro-pre (72.74 1.94) all have overlapping 95% Macro CIs. The Macro ranking among the top three is not statistically distinguishable on the basis of accuracy alone. This motivates our reliance on Bradley-Terry Elo with bootstrap CIs (Appendix B.3), which pools 633,252 pairwise comparisons across questions and places claude-opus-4-6 30 Elo points above gemini-3.1-pro-pre with non-overlapping CIs. Micro CIs are roughly half as wide as Macro CIs. Micro CI half-widths are 0.801.20% across all 30 models, while Macro half-widths are 1.942.56%. The 2 gap reflects that Macro averages 16 per-asset proportions while Micro pools all 5,000 questions. As consequence, the top-3 Micro CIs do not overlap (e.g., claude-opus-4-6 at 77.76 1.00 versus gemini-3.1-pro-pre at 75.92 1.02). Future evaluations of DiagnosticIQ should report both metrics, with Macro reflecting per-class generalization and Micro reflecting overall accuracy precision.
